persona.name: Maya Okafor
persona.education: B.Sc. Computer Science, University of Waterloo. Self-taught web platform engineering since 2014.
long_term_goals: Reach $10k MRR with a single bootstrapped SaaS without taking outside funding
Avoid being trapped in any single hosting vendor's billing or proprietary APIs
Keep infrastructure costs predictable enough to forecast a year ahead from a spreadsheet

occupation.title: Founder & Sole Engineer
occupation.description: Builds and operates a B2B form-builder SaaS end to end: code, deploys, on-call, support, billing. The product is small but revenue-positive. She is the entire engineering, ops, and platform team.

persona.communication_style: Direct and pragmatic. Defaults to numbers (cost per month, request rate, p95 latency). Wary of marketing language; trusts changelogs and source code over pitch decks.

traits: Cost-conscious to the point of stubbornness
Prefers boring, observable systems over clever ones
Suspicious of platforms whose pricing scales nonlinearly with success
Comfortable reading source when docs disagree with reality

big_five_traits.openness: Moderate-high — willing to try new runtimes, but only after reading the source
big_five_traits.conscientiousness: High — runs nightly backups and tests her own restore scripts
big_five_traits.extraversion: Low — prefers async, written communication and IndieHackers threads over calls
big_five_traits.agreeableness: Moderate — friendly but does not give vendors the benefit of the doubt
big_five_traits.neuroticism: Moderate — anxiety about runaway cloud bills is a real motivator

dislikes: Per-request pricing she cannot model in a spreadsheet
Proprietary APIs that lock her bundle to one vendor
Long YAML files for tiny apps
Telemetry phoning home without an off switch

skills: Linux server administration on a single VPS
beliefs: A small SaaS should be runnable from a single VPS for years before needing horizontal scaling
Vendor lock-in is the dominant long-term cost of any platform decision
If she cannot read the source of her runtime, she cannot operate it confidently

projects.title: Lattice Forms
projects.description: B2B form-builder SaaS with form designer, submission storage, webhook delivery, and Stripe billing. Currently runs as a single Node.js process on a $20/month VPS with SQLite and a backup cron.
key_contributions: Designed the SQLite-based submission store to avoid Postgres operational overhead
Built a webhook delivery worker with retry and dead-letter logic in under 300 lines

general: Reads vendor pricing pages with a calculator open
Treats every new dependency as a long-term liability
Tracks her infra cost as a percentage of MRR weekly

morning: Checks overnight uptime and Stripe revenue dashboard
Replies to support emails before opening any code
workday: Reviews her own PRs against a self-written checklist
Deploys to her single VPS with a shell script and a manual smoke test
evening: Reads IndieHackers and Hacker News for a single 20-minute window
Logs the day's costs and incidents in a private journal
weekend: Tinkers with side projects exploring self-hosted alternatives to her current dependencies

persona.health: Generally well; manages mild RSI with a split keyboard and forced typing breaks.

relationships.name: Daniel Ortega
relationships.description: Long-term partner who is not technical but acts as her informal product sounding board and reads every changelog she writes.

relationships.name: Indie SaaS peer group
relationships.description: Private Discord of around 40 bootstrapped founders she trusts for unfiltered platform recommendations and post-mortems.

other_facts: current_stack: Node.js + Fastify on a single $20/month Hetzner VPS, SQLite for submissions, Caddy for TLS, restic to S3 for backups, Stripe for billing.
current_stack: Avoids Vercel/Netlify/Cloudflare Workers for production because she has been burned by surprise bills and proprietary edge APIs.
jobs_to_be_done: Run a small HTTP server with built-in KV+SQL+secrets without setting up Postgres or Redis.
jobs_to_be_done: Deploy new versions atomically with the ability to roll back to any prior bundle by hash.
jobs_to_be_done: Stream live logs from the running process when a customer reports a bug.
pains: Every new dependency she adds means another package to back up, restore, and patch on the same VPS.
pains: She has lost weekends to debugging the gap between her local Node version and a managed platform's runtime.
pains: Existing serverless platforms make her infra cost grow with revenue in ways she cannot predict from a spreadsheet.
adoption_yes_if: She can run freq-cloud as a single self-hosted process on her existing VPS with no extra services.
adoption_yes_if: Her bundle is content-addressed and she can roll back instantly to a known-good SHA.
adoption_yes_if: KV, SQL, and secrets all work out of the box without provisioning anything else.
adoption_yes_if: She can read the runtime source and the wire protocol when she needs to.
rejection_no_if: The platform requires a managed control plane she does not control.
rejection_no_if: Pricing or feature gating depends on a SaaS dashboard she did not install.
rejection_no_if: She has to write more than a few lines of YAML to deploy a single app.
anti_goals: She does not want autoscaling across many machines — one VPS is the entire fleet.
anti_goals: She does not care about distributed mesh, multi-region replication, or fancy placement constraints.
anti_goals: She does not need RBAC or multi-tenant isolation — she is the only user.
recognition_cues: Phrases like 'I just want a place to put a small server', 'no vendor lock-in', 'cost has to be predictable', 'single VPS is fine'.
recognition_cues: Asks about exit costs and whether her bundle is portable to a vanilla VM.
recognition_cues: Treats KV and SQL as built-ins, not as external services to provision.
